title: Core Concepts in Consciousness
body:
A useful way to think about consciousness is to separate experience from the machinery that supports it. Qualia are the felt qualities of experience: the redness of red, the sting of pain, the taste of salt. They are what make consciousness seem so hard to reduce to objective description.

Researchers also talk about neural correlates of consciousness, the patterns of activity that reliably track awareness. These correlates do not by themselves solve the hard problem, but they provide a scientific foothold. If a certain type of brain activity appears whenever a subject reports being aware of something, that activity becomes a target for explanation.

The leading theories each emphasise a different mechanism. Integrated Information Theory focuses on the structure of causal integration. Global Workspace Theory focuses on global access and broadcasting. Higher-order theories focus on a system representing its own states. These are not merely abstract disagreements; they imply very different research programs.

Other important ideas include the binding problem, which asks how the brain merges sight, sound, touch, and memory into one unified experience, and the explanatory gap, which names the distance between physical description and lived feeling. Altered states like sleep, anaesthesia, dreams, and psychedelic experiences are crucial because they reveal that consciousness is not one fixed thing. It changes in structure, intensity, and content.
keyPoints: Qualia are the subjective qualities of experience
Neural correlates of consciousness are measurable brain patterns associated with awareness
Integrated Information Theory ties consciousness to integrated causal structure
Global Workspace Theory explains consciousness through broadcast and access
Higher-order theories say awareness requires the brain to represent its own states
The binding problem asks how separate sensory streams become one experience
The explanatory gap names the difficulty of linking physics to feeling
Altered states are essential evidence because they show consciousness can change in systematic ways
furtherReading: 'The Rediscovery of the Mind' by John Searle
'Attention Schema Theory' papers by Michael Graziano
'The Brain and the Inner World' by Mark Solms
